About Me
Background

I'm Antony Ganapolskiy, a sophomore Mechanical Engineering student at San Jose State University with a focus on automotive systems and precision manufacturing. I'm drawn to the full lifecycle of a part — from CAD model to finished, machined component.

My hands-on experience spans SolidWorks design, manual lathe and mill operation, embedded systems programming, and automotive restoration. I currently work at Rivian as a Product Guide, applying my engineering knowledge daily to explain EV powertrain and software systems to customers.

I'm always looking to take on challenging projects and contribute to teams that build things with care and precision.

School & Engineering
Engineering Projects

Hands-on projects from SJSU coursework — designed in SolidWorks and manufactured on real shop equipment.

Project 01 — Lathe & Mill
Precision Mechanical Fabrication

A two-part machining project requiring both lathe and vertical mill work. Each component was designed in SolidWorks with formal shop drawings, then manufactured to tight tolerances and verified with calipers and micrometers.

The milled aluminum plate was designed to house a 10-24 machine screw. This meant the tapped center hole required a precise diameter — too large and the screw strips; too small and it won't thread. Holding the ±0.005" tolerance demanded careful speed/feed selection and incremental passes to reach final dimension.

// Shop Drawings
Drawing 01 — Lathe: Hex bolt with chamfer callouts
Drawing 02 — Mill: Aluminum plate with tapped hole & countersinks
// Fabricated Parts

Disassembled — plate, bolt & machine screw

RadiusR.25" pocket
Tolerance±0.005"

Project 02 — Autonomous Systems
Autonomous Robotics — Coding Lead

Led software integration for a fully autonomous robot using RobotC, coordinating across mechanical and electrical subteams. Responsible for sensor logic, motor control, and ensuring software reliability throughout the full system.

RobotCAutonomous SystemsSystems IntegrationTeam LeadSensor LogicMotor Control
Project 03 — Embedded Systems
Reaction-Time Game — CircuitPython

Built a microcontroller-based reaction-time game on an Adafruit Circuit Playground board programmed in CircuitPython. The built-in NeoPixel LEDs act as visual stimuli and feedback, while onboard buttons capture responses. Implemented real-time event handling, timing logic, and multi-color LED feedback.

Adafruit Circuit Playground — NeoPixel LEDs active during game operation
CircuitPythonEmbedded SystemsAdafruit Circuit PlaygroundNeoPixel LEDsReal-Time LogicMicrocontroller

Automotive Projects
Build Timeline

Hands-on automotive work done outside of school — wrenching, fabricating, and building in the garage.

2024 — Sold Summer 2024
Mazda MX-5 NC1 — Restoration & Build
SOLD — Summer 2024

Purchased the NC1 as a project car in rough shape — missing a headlight, damaged fender, and electrical issues. Sorted it mechanically, fixed the body damage, and gave it some personality before selling it.

Step 01 — Acquisition
As Purchased — Missing Headlight & Fender Damage

Came with a missing driver-side headlight assembly — socket and wiring harness corroded and cut. Fender had damage needing attention before cosmetic work.

As acquired — missing driver headlight, fender damage visible
Project AssessmentParts SourcingDamage Evaluation
Step 02 — Teardown
Front End Disassembly

Pulled the front bumper cover, headlight buckets, and surrounding trim to access the wiring and fender. Car also went up on the lift for an underside inspection.

Bumper off, hood open — assessing front end
On the lift — front clip removed for access
Front End DisassemblyLift WorkInspection
Step 03 — Electrical
Headlight Replacement & Wiring Harness Repair

The original headlight wiring harness was corroded and improperly cut. Sourced a replacement headlight and soldered a new wiring harness from scratch — heat-shrinking every connection. Identified the NC1 pin-out, matched wire gauges, and verified low beam, high beam, and DRL before closing up.

Wiring Harness FabricationSolderingElectrical DiagnosisHeat ShrinkHeadlight Assembly
Step 04 — Body Work
Salvage Fender Sourcing & Replacement

Located a matching replacement fender at a local salvage yard — same body color, minimal damage. Swapped it onto the car and realigned panel gaps against the hood and door before final torquing.

Salvage Yard SourcingPanel ReplacementPanel Gap AlignmentBody Work
Step 05 — Reassembly
Front End Complete — First Wash

With the headlight wired, fender fitted, and bumper back on, the front end was solid and complete.

First wash after reassembly — headlights wired and working
ReassemblyFitment CheckDetail
Step 06 — Cosmetics
Hood Wrap — Gloss Green Vinyl

Wrapped the hood in gloss green vinyl to break up the grey body and give the car a more aggressive, custom look before it was sold.

Hood wrap in progress — gloss green vinyl laid
Wrap complete — two-tone grey & green
Vinyl WrapBody PrepTwo-Tone Custom
